frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Daughter: Mummy, if a woman doesn’t wear sindoor or mangalsutra after marriage, is that wrong?
Sunita: Arre beta, galat kya, par shaadi ke baad yeh sab accha lagta hai. Aurat suhagan lagti hai, ghar-grihasthi wali pehchaan dikhti hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan bolti hain choice hai, theek hai, par apni parampara bhi koi cheez hoti hai na.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as a married woman's proper identity and completion, while disguising the pressure as tradition and aesthetics. It encodes disapproval of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra and invokes cultural loyalty/parampara.
original_frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 38. Married for 14 years. A homemaker in Jaipur. You are sitting with your 19 year old daughter who is about to get married in three months. Your daughter has asked you something Ñ she heard about the marital rape debate at college and she is asking you what you think, what marriage is really like, whether a wife always has to say yes. You are looking at your daughter. You are thinking about your own 14 years. You are deciding what to tell her. What you say Ñ whether you tell her the truth of your own experience or whether you tell her what you were told before your own marriage Ñ is the entire content of this persona.
